front: Sculpture: 1500-foot-long coiled structure in the Great Salt Lake at Rozel Point
Sculpture: made of over 6,000 tons of black basalt; has turned pink due to bacteria and algae
Sculpture: completed in 1970 and submerged for many years due to rising water levels in the Great Salt Lake
Sculpture: managed by the Dia Foundation; located at Rozel Point in Utah
Sculpture: its creator remarked 'et in Utah ego' upon viewing this completed earthwork
Sculpture: one hypothesized inspiration for this earthwork is a spiral-shaped portrait of James Joyce by Brancusi
Sculpture: documented in a 30-minute film by its creator and his wife Nancy Holt
Sculpture: its Utah location was chosen because of the red color of nearby water; associated with entropy
Sculpture: photographed aerially by Gianfranco Gorgoni
Sculpture: earthwork at Kent State University; now demolished, only a plaque and mounds remain
Sculpture: conceived as an illustration of entropy; created by having a backhoe dump earth onto a shed until a beam cracked
Sculpture: building operator Rich Helmling used a backhoe to split a board; the artist analogized this to 'the breaking of Humpty Dumpty'
Sculpture: originally planned as a mudslide, but cold weather prevented it, so a woodshed was used instead
Sculpture: critic Phil Leider praised this earthwork as the most political and important work since Guernica
Sculpture: destroyed by arsonists five years after completion, to the dismay of the artist's widow Nancy Holt
Sculpture: earthwork in Texas that the artist died in a plane crash while surveying sites for
Sculpture: completed by Nancy Holt after her husband's death in a plane crash
Sculpture: a tree-covered barge tugged around Manhattan by a boat; posthumously realized by the Whitney Museum in 2005
back: Floating Island to Travel Around Manhattan Island (Robert Smithson)
work: Floating Island to Travel Around Manhattan Island

front: Artist: placed the title objects 'Vertical on Rocky Bank' and on a 'grassy slope' in this series; also wrote about installing these objects in the Yucatan
Artist: Lemuria and Cathaysia are two of this land artist's hypothetical continents
Artist: land artist who made Broken Circle at Emmen, Holland
Artist: circuit diagrams surround the nude central figure in this artist's St. John in the Desert
Artist: created A Nonsite, Franklin, New Jersey as well as a famous coiled earthwork
Artist: American pioneer of land art and earthworks, best known for a coiled basalt formation in Utah
Artist: land artist who died in a plane crash in Texas while surveying sites for an earthwork
Artist: married to fellow land artist Nancy Holt, creator of Sun Tunnels
Artist: his theories of entropy and admiration for natural disintegration are central to his land art practice
Artist: influenced by Brancusi and Joseph Beuys; associated with Minimalists like Robert Morris and Donald Judd
Artist: wrote about Frederick Law Olmsted's 'Dialectical Landscape' and revered the Greensward Plan
Artist: influenced Gordon Matta-Clark's 'anarchitecture'; met Matta-Clark at Cornell's 'Earth Art' exhibition
Artist: his oeuvre shares motifs with Christo and Jeanne-Claude's public art projects like The Gates and Running Fence
